coredata.dc:description: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have gained widespread usage in 5G and edge computing systems due to their simplicity, cost-effectiveness, lightweight nature, and ease of maintenance. However, these batteries consume a substantial amount of energy, making effective capacity management a critical demand. Consequently, numerous studies have concentrated on the state-of-health (SoH) prediction of LIBs to enable efficient power management and deployment. Recent research often employs sequence models to forecast SoH, leveraging the analysis of charging and discharging cycles from historical data. However, battery information inherently contains channel-wise data, which poses challenges in the representation using sequence models. Additionally, the presence of peak capacity in the cycles during the lifecycle suggests that the relationship from cycle-wise information may be more complex than linear temporal aggregation. In this paper, we propose a graph-based approach to deal with LIB SoH forecasting. Specifically, we present a novel model called the Multiview Nested Graph Convolutional Network (MvNGCN), which leverages two Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) models to capture features. Unlike the sequence-based approach, the GCN creates accurate and stable forecasting outputs. Experimental evaluation on public benchmarking datasets from NASA and CALCE demonstrates that MvNGCN offers significant performance improvements. We reduce the overall RMSE and MAPE losses at 7.91% and 16.78%, respectively on the NASA dataset compared to the baseline. Similarly, we also decrease 2.92% and 5.09% on the CALCE dataset. To enhance reproducibility, we have released our source code as an open repository available at https://github.com/bdachung/mvngcn (accessed: 23 September 2024).

head.ce:title: Towards Multiple-View Nested Graph Convolutional Networks for stable and robust lithium-ion battery forecasting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Computer Science and Engineering, Ho Chi Minh City University of Technology (HCMUT), Ho Chi Minh City, Viet Nam

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have gained widespread usage in 5G and edge computing systems due to their simplicity, cost-effectiveness, lightweight nature, and ease of maintenance. However, these batteries consume a substantial amount of energy, making effective capacity management a critical demand. Consequently, numerous studies have concentrated on the state-of-health (SoH) prediction of LIBs to enable efficient power management and deployment. Recent research often employs sequence models to forecast SoH, leveraging the analysis of charging and discharging cycles from historical data. However, battery information inherently contains channel-wise data, which poses challenges in the representation using sequence models. Additionally, the presence of peak capacity in the cycles during the lifecycle suggests that the relationship from cycle-wise information may be more complex than linear temporal aggregation. In this paper, we propose a graph-based approach to deal with LIB SoH forecasting. Specifically, we present a novel model called the Multiview Nested Graph Convolutional Network (MvNGCN), which leverages two Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) models to capture features. Unlike the sequence-based approach, the GCN creates accurate and stable forecasting outputs. Experimental evaluation on public benchmarking datasets from NASA and CALCE demonstrates that MvNGCN offers significant performance improvements. We reduce the overall RMSE and MAPE losses at 7.91% and 16.78%, respectively on the NASA dataset compared to the baseline. Similarly, we also decrease 2.92% and 5.09% on the CALCE dataset. To enhance reproducibility, we have released our source code as an open repository available at https://github.com/bdachung/mvngcn (accessed: 23 September 2024).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: LIB are widely recognized as reliable power supplies for various mobility applications. They are preferred over traditional acid-lead batteries due to their proven stability, high performance, cost-effectiveness, lightweight nature, and compact size. However, as the demand for advanced technologies such as big data, 5G, and flying devices increases, the tolerance for errors must be minimized. Practical applications of LIBs necessitate continuous health tracking to detect potential faults, issue warnings, and facilitate timely replacements. To address this requirement, the use of smart batteries (SLIB) (Gemma, 2018), capable of accurately forecasting the health of LIBs, becomes essential. Consequently, precise forecasting of the health status of LIBs is critical for maintaining the overall integrity of the system.

In a LIB system, several parameters are commonly monitored, including the battery’s State-of-Charge (SoC), State-of-Health (SoH) and Remaining-Useful-Life (RUL) (Xu et al., 2019). As a result of the interrelationships among these three variables, this paper primarily focuses on examining the SoH. Essentially, SoH reflects the battery’s current status relative to its ideal quality, representing its capacity. The capacity is computed by dividing the current maximum available capacity, denoted as
C
f u l l
, by the initial value provided by the vendors, given by
C
i n i t
, as shown below.
(1)
S o H =
C
f u l l
C
i n i t
× 100 %

Therefore, the prediction of the LIB’s SoH also involves estimating the battery’s capacity, denoted as
C
f u l l
. In practical usage, the
C
f u l l
of LIBs degrades over time with each cycle, resulting in an overall downward trend in the time-series data. However, due to variations in actual usage and specific electrochemical performance, the battery’s capacity degradation follows a non-linear pattern (see Fig. 1), which adds giant complexity to SoH estimation. Despite these challenges, numerous methods have been proposed to achieve accurate and stable predictions of future cycles LIB capacities.

Previous studies have offered various approaches to tackle the problem of SoH estimation. These approaches can be generally divided into two main groups: (1) model-based and event-driven methods (Goebel et al., 2008; Daigle and Kulkarni, 2016), and (2) data-driven methods (Zhao et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2018; Song et al., 2018; Khalid et al., 2019). The former approach relies on an understanding of the internal organization and long-term behavior of batteries, as well as historical events that affect battery performance. In contrast, the latter approach provides less domain-knowledge explanation but excels in extracting dependencies from actual data collected from devices. As a result of the technology explosion in Artificial Intelligence (AI), particularly Deep Learning (DL) approaches, data-driven methods have emerged and gained immense popularity (Cornegruta et al., 2016; Abdul-Mageed and Ungar, 2017; Park et al., 2020). Currently, the nested sequence model in Autoregression with Nested Sequence (ARNS) (Pham et al., 2022) and the Convolutional Transformer with Nested Sequence Models (CTARNS) (Bui et al., 2023) have been recognized as the state-of-the-art approaches for addressing the issue at hand.

However, these methods still rely on sequence-based techniques such as Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) or Transformer, wherein the forecasting action mostly leverages the sequential relation from the charging and discharging cycles of LIB. Presenting the information using a sequence-based structure does not fully and naturally reflect the characteristics of LIB data. In fact, the features within LIB functional cycles comprise information at both channel and cycle levels. Channel-wise information includes temperature, current, voltage, or internal resistance during the charging cycle. On the other hand, cycle-wise information refers to data collected during a complete cycle. Obviously, relations between channel-wise factors can hardly be regarded as a sequential (or linear) relationship. Furthermore, empirical evidence about cycle-wise information reveals that the relationships between cycles are naturally non-linear, especially during peak cycles (which will be discussed in Section 2.2.2, peak/non-peak indicators). As a result, forecasting cannot solely rely on analyzing the linear dependencies of historical cycles but requires a more sophisticated approach. Moreover, in our experiments with large LIB datasets, statistics show that the initial weights used for training the sequence models are usually initialized randomly. Noticeably, this leads to major differences in the models’ accuracy output. This poses significant challenges when applying the models to practical and confidential applications.

Recently, Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs) have shown significant improvements in end-to-end models by capturing spatial dependencies in various applications. In the work (Kipf and Welling, 2017), the theory behind leveraging mathematical foundations for graph convolutional models was presented. A survey conducted in Wu et al. (2019) categorizes Graph Neural Network(GNN) publications into four main categories: Recurrent GNN, Convolutional GNN, Graph Autoencoders, and Spatial–temporal GNN (STemGNN). Among these categories, forecasting LIB SoH belongs to the group of STemGNNs. In the study by Cao et al. (2021), GCNs were utilized to enhance the forecasting output for multivariate time-series data. This technique, known for its dynamic nature, is capable of incorporating spatial–temporal aggregation in diverse datasets. In this paper, we introduce a robust yet stable GCN method, known as Multi-view Nested Graph Convolutional Networks (MvNGCN), tailored for LIB SoH forecasting and demonstrate its improvement compared to the latest studies reported on LIB datasets.

From an architectural perspective, MvNGCN is developed based on ARNS and CTARNS models presented in Pham et al. (2022) and Bui et al. (2023). The core idea is to leverage nested sequence-to-sequence (Seq2Seq) or sequence-in-sequence (SeqInSeq) models, which encode historical data into contextual information. This information is then passed to the decoder for generating the final forecasting. In the encoder organization, we propose the innovative use of the Local Convolutional Transformer Decoder (Local CT-De) architecture, employing the 1D Dilated Casual CNN module to aggregate cause-and-result relationships from past cycles and encoded contextual information. Notably, to decode information in the prediction pipeline, we represent it using a graph structure rather than a merely sequence-based format, which enables us to better capture its characteristics. Furthermore, we introduce a novel approach involving two views of GCN called as Channel-view and Cycle-view. The Channel-view GCN is designed to support capturing graph-based channel-wise information, while the Cycle-view GCN focuses on cycle-wise information. The use of these two views helps precisely describe the semantics data of LIB, leading to a more robust forecast performance.

Moreover, the weight distribution initialization during training creates a message passing mechanism for the GCN views to aggregate node information from their neighboring nodes. As a result, the specific variance distribution for the training weights is small, reducing the impact of random weight initialization issues. Our experiments on NASA (Saha and Goebel, 2007) and CALCE (Pecht, 2013) datasets show that we consistently demonstrate better performance in comparison to the state-of-the-art results reported in ARNS (Pham et al., 2022) and CTARNS (Bui et al., 2023), considered as baseline in our study.

Therefore, based on the existing methods for LIB SoH forecasting, we propose the MvNGCN to leverage the graph-based dependencies of data into a sophisticated model, aiming to create high-accuracy and stable forecasting outputs. Such main contributions of this paper can be listed as follows.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Robustness proven: Given that battery data follows a SeqInSeq dependencies and share major neighboring interconnection of nodes in a graph, we developed MvNGCN with a data-driven topology. This graph fully leverages the message passing between nodes in both channel-wise and cycle-wise dimensions. By doing so, our approach significantly enhances the robustness of the output, consistently achieving very low loss and high accuracy.

Reliability proven: Notably, in a GCN, the weight distribution initialization during training produces a message propagation to aggregate node information from neighboring nodes. Unlike the sequence-based mechanisms of existing methods, our graph model maintains a small variance in weight distribution during message passing. This minimizes the impact of random weight initialization compared to other methods in the literature. As a result, our model consistently delivers stable outcomes across multiple runs with different scenarios.

ce:section.ce:para: The rest of this paper is structured as follows. Section 2 presents an overview of the preliminaries necessary to comprehend the problem and the methodology. Section 3 explains a discussion on related works, which serve as the foundation for formulating the SoH prediction models. In Section 4, we present our proposed method called MvNGCN and outline the improvements we have made compared to existing studies in the literature. Section 5 presents the experimental results and comparison of the outputs. Eventually, Section 6 concludes the paper.

The first aspect that requires introduction is the Lithium-ion datasets. LIBs generally operate based on the fundamental principles of charging and discharging. In this study, we evaluated our methods using the well-known NASA and CALCE datasets, which are commonly utilized in the literature. These batteries are operated under strict accuracy measurement requirements and are tested in laboratory conditions, adhering to the popular CC–CV (Constant Current–Constant Voltage) charging principle.

Typically, a LIB operates in a continuous loop of different operating profiles until its capacity degrades to a specified limit. This special period is often referred to as the point where SoH decreases to 70% of the rated capacity. Generally, the operating profiles include charging, discharging, and impedance periods. Consequently, LIBs are designed to function based on the CC–CV principle. Fig. 2 illustrates an example of this procedure for a LIB from the NASA dataset. In the figure, the “Charge cycle” occurs under CC mode at the rate of 1.5 A until the battery voltage attains 4.2 V. Subsequently, it transitions to the CV period as long as the charging current drops down to 20 mA. In CC mode, the discharging cycle is performed until the battery voltage reduces to 2.7 V for battery #5. The behaviors are set similarly at 2.5 V, 2.2 V, and 2.5 V for batteries #6, #7, and #18 of the NASA dataset.

In this study, we assess our hypothesis using the NASA dataset (Saha and Goebel, 2007), which contains four batteries #5, #6, #7, and #18, as well as the CALCE dataset (Pecht, 2013), which includes batteries #35, #36, #37, and #38. These datasets contain LIBs with CC–CV working profiles, making them suitable for our evaluation. In the NASA dataset, the LIBs operate under a stable current of 1.5 A while the voltage continuously increases until reaching the limit of 4.2 V during the charging session. In the discharging session, a constant voltage is maintained while the current drops until reaching 20 mA. In the NASA dataset, the internal cycle information recorded includes voltage, current, and temperature. However, in the CALCE dataset, cycle temperature data is not given, so the internal resistance is used as the replacement.

Analogously, the CALCE dataset (Pecht, 2013) has the configuration of 1.1Ah as the
C
r a t e d
capacity and also follows the CC–CV principle. The discharging current is 1.1 A, and the batteries undergo testing within the room temperature. There are four batteries, #35, #36, #37, and #38, similar to that of the NASA dataset. The CALCE crucial information in a channel would be voltage, current, and resistance. The data summarization of these datasets are listed in Tables 1 and 2.

The potential input features consist of input internally within a cycle (channel-wise attributes) and input from the cycle itself (cycle-wise attributes). The channel-wise attributes concept was introduced in Wu et al. (2016) and the cycle-wise data was presented in Hu et al. (2015). In this section, we will get into more detail about them.

Typically, a LIB undergoes three stages: charging, discharging, and relaxing. Throughout each cycle, different internal aspects of the LIB, such as current, voltage, temperature, or internal resistance, are recorded. However, the volume of multi-channel data recorded for each cycle is enormous. Hence, a sampling mechanism is necessary. In the discharging process, the rapidly changing current flowing through the LIB significantly affects the measurement of input data. Therefore, we exclude the discharging data from the channel-wise data. Similarly, during the relaxing process, the data changes at a very slow rate. As a result, we also exclude the relaxing data from the channel-wise data. Consequently, we exclusively collect data during the charging period, which produces three feature vectors with a length of S and represented for a channel-wise input as

Important sampling technique proposed in Wu et al. (2016) suggests sampling 11 featuring points during the CC period, recorded at the position where percentages reach 35, 52, 70, 88, 90, 92, 94, 96, 98, 99, 100 of the overall CC charging timeline. Every such point comprises of 3 features, namely voltage, current, temperature for NASA dataset and voltage, current, temperature for CALCE dataset. Due to its efficiency in Wu et al. (2016), the mentioned sampling technique is also employed in this paper. An example of these 11 data points is displayed in Fig. 3.

Besides the channel-wise features mentioned above, we introduce important features extracted from a cycle additionally. This approach is derived from the method first introduced in Hu et al. (2015). The five features are outlined below and visualized in Fig. 4.

x

: Voltage value at the end of charging process.

x

: Current value at the end of charging process.

Furthermore, we introduce the feature peak/non-peak indicator, denoted as P, which represents the status that battery capacity creates a peak. P is derived by comparing the present capacity value with the mean of that value of the preceding five cycles. This assessment helps to determine if the considering capacity is a non-peak or peak value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and its advanced variants
ce:section.ce:para: CNN has been extensively utilized for encoding time series inputs (Kiranyaz et al., 2021). In this section, we will focus on describing the variant known as the 1D Convolutional Neural Network (1DConv), which is essential for the subsequent content.

The 1DConv layer operates on a 3D tensor input with dimensions of
( N ,
L
i n
,
D
i n
)
, where N depicts the batch size,
L
i n
corresponds to the input sequence length, and
D
i n
is expressed as the input channel quantity. The resulting output of the 1DConv layer is likewise a 3D tensor with dimensions
( N ,
L
o u t
,
D
o u t
)
. Here,
L
o u t
represents the output sequence length, while
D
o u t
is used to express the output channel quantity. The convolution operation is depicted in Fig. 5, where a kernel slides over the input, and each element of the output is determined through the dot product between the kernel and a portion of the input.

It can be noticed that while the input remains unchanged, the output of the 1DConv layer will have a shorter length due to the convolution operation. Therefore, zero-padding is used to ensure the identical sequence length of the input and output. In cases where the input has multiple channels (i.e.,
D
i n
> 1
), the kernel shape will be (
k e r n e l _ s i z e ,
D
i n
), and the convolution operation will proceed similar to the description provided in Fig. 5.

1D Convolutional Layer has been used in many time-series problems due to its advantages in low computational efficiency and simple convolutional operation (Kiranyaz et al., 2021). As a result, this model is utilized in IoT and real-time low-level hardware applications.

Dilated Convolution (DConv) (Yu and Koltun, 2016) is employed to increase the effective kernel size in 1DConv while maintaining equal parameter volume. Therefore, it broadened the receptive field efficiently. Through this mechanism, the structure captures a broader range of data over an extended period, providing better input without increasing computation cost. Fig. 6 gives an example with dilation values from 1 to 3 and a DConv Layer applying a kernel (size = 3). For the dilation 1, 2, 3, we have the receptive field sizes are 3, 5 and 7 respectively.

The Causal Convolution (CConv) Layer is formulated to handle temporal data, as it maintains the temporal order during the operation of convolution. This property allows the current value to depend on past data without knowledge of the future. To implement the CConv Layer, zero-padding values are added to the input tensor. In the problem of time series forecasting, the input and output typically share the same length. To ensure causality in a kernel size k of a convolution layer and dilation factor d, we add
d ×
( k − 1 )
zeros at the beginning of the input. Fig. 7 illustrates an example with
d = 1
and
k = 3
dilation and kernel size, respectively.

1D Dilated Causal Convolutional Neural Network or Temporal Convolutional Network (TConv) was originally introduced in WaveNet (Oord et al., 2016). In this architecture, TConv incorporates CConv and DConv to leverage the models on causality and receptive field expansion. An example of TConv is given in Fig. 8.

The Pointwise Convolutional (PConv) Layer is a variant of 1DConv that utilizes a kernel size of 1 × 1 to process each individual point (Kim and Kim, 2022). It operates similarly to a Fully Connected Layer (FCL), where each value is multiplied by a single weight. However, unlike the FCL, the PConv preserves the input shape while altering the output channels and takes advantage of the spatial information present in multidimensional inputs. This distinction between the PConv and FCL is illustrated in Fig. 9. In the image, it can be observed that the FCL does not retain historical input information, while the PConv encodes spatial information and adjusts the output size (i.e., the channel quantity).

The ConvTrans architecture proposed in Kim and Kim (2022) for multivariate time series prediction has the overall architecture in Fig. 10. It follows Encoder–Decoder architecture like the original Transformer presented in Vaswani et al. (2017). In this scale, the input has the shape
( B , N , T )
, where B depicts the batch size, N represents the number of variables and T is the length of a time series data.

Encoder: The encoder in the organization includes a stack of N identically structured layers. Every layer has 3 sub-layers (Self-Attention, PConv and TConv layers). The architect leverages a residual connection between two consecutive layers of the three sub-layers, followed by layer normalization (Ba et al., 2016). Stated differently, the result of the sub-layer is
L a y e r N o r m
( x + S u b l a y e r
( x )
)
, with
S u b l a y e r
( x )
representing the function executed by the sub-layer.

Decoder: The decoder is also a stack of N identically structured layers. Besides, each layer consists of the same 3 sub-layers of the encoder but with different arrangements (TConv, Self-Attention and PConv). However, the keys and values of the Self-Attention mechanism in the decoder come from the previous encoder output.

As discussed in the subsequent sections, ConvTrans has demonstrated its effectiveness in encoding features, which we will leverage in our method in the following sections as part of our contributions.

ce:section.ce:para: Among the existing GCN (Wu et al., 2019), there are two notable approaches: spectral and spatial paths. The spectral approach defines convolution as a graph Fourier transformation, whereas the spatial approach defines convolution as message passing between neighboring nodes. These two paths represent popular strategies in graph convolution and have been widely studied in the literature.

A major type of GCNs is the Spectral Graph Convolution Network, which was introduced in Kipf and Welling (2017). The unnormalized Laplacian matrix is represented by Eq. (8), and the spectral convolution operation is illustrated in Eq. (9).

Spectral-based approaches have a long history in graph theory, and there have been significant advancements in approximating these methods to reduce computational inefficiencies. Recently, the link between graph theory and time-series studies has made GCN a trending topic in the area. In the following, we will explain the next approach, which is the spatial-based GCN.

Spatial Graph Convolutional Network approaches draw inspiration from Recurrent Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to establish graph convolutions using the propagating information. After (Kipf and Welling, 2017) connected the gap between spectral and spatial studies, spatial-based methods become a hot topic for discussion recently due to their flexibility, robustness, and generality. This type of network involves two steps: message passing and aggregation, as illustrated in Fig. 11(a).

During the message passing step, the feature vectors of each node in the neighborhood of node v are refined before being sent to node v following this process.

Moreover, by stacking GCN layers, each node in the graph can gather information from remote nodes, thereby expanding its receptive field. This process is illustrated in Fig. 11(b) with a more detailed depiction. However, excessively stacking GCN layers can lead to an over-smoothing issue. In this case, the feature vectors of all nodes in the graph become highly similar, potentially diminishing the discriminative power of the model.

Recently, there has been much interest in this topic, creating its section in the taxonomy tree of in the domain Wu et al. (2019). One notable contribution in this field is the introduction of the Spectral Temporal GNN (STemGNN) in Cao et al. (2021), which leverages the combination of multivariate time-series inputs of different data types. The total design is illustrated in Fig. 12. In this model, StemGNN Block 1 and 2 incorporate extensions such as 1DConv and Graph Fourier Transform (GFT). To enhance the training performance, the model utilizes output losses for both forecast and backcast branches. The paper demonstrates the strengths of this architect across various multivariate time-series datasets. Therefore it is a natural progression to leverage this model into our LIB forecasting problem.

Normally, the LIB is typically collected using two timescales: calendar time
t
s
and the index of cycles
t
c
(Xu et al., 2019). Each cycle aligns with a specific calendar timescale
t
s
. Fig. 13 illustrates the capacity regeneration process, showing how it manifests as a gap between two cycles that exceeds a certain threshold (Pan et al., 2021).

In literature, many studies have explored the correlation between the “rest period” and LIB capacity regeneration, known as the “relaxation effect”. For instance, in Rashid and Gupta (2015), it was demonstrated that rest duration allows Lithium-ion cells to recover after discharge and charge. This contributes to the improved utilization of cyclable Lithium-ion and enhances battery capacity regeneration. Additionally, a long relaxation period upon the completion of the discharging phase makes the negative electrode in the solid matrix have a higher concentration than the positive one. This leads to a higher potential mode of the cells, which significantly impacts battery performance in subsequent cycles. Obviously, relaxation after charging has a marginal effect both practically and what we can see in the recorded capacities in Fig. 13.

Furthermore, Reichert et al. (2013) showed that the amount of regenerated capacity is non-linear and the relaxation factor plays a more essential impact compared to defining cycle capacity. In this paper, we leverage the rest period as an important cycle-wise feature in our 6-tuple feature vector. This is expected to enhance the prediction of LIB SoH, particularly during peak periods.

As discussed in Section 1, model-based and event-based methods for LIB SoH forecasting rely heavily on domain knowledge of LIB and often suffer from computational inefficiency, their main drawback lies in their limited output accuracy. In a historical context, the paper (Goebel et al., 2008) utilized an electrochemical model to predict capacity by modeling the relationship between capacity and internal impedance based on their negative linear dependencies. This impedance was then measured by using an electrochemical spectrometry technique. Additionally, in Daigle and Kulkarni (2016), a predictive aging model was employed to estimate active parameters of LIB batteries based on internal impedance and diffusion coefficients.

More recently, an interesting event-driven approach (Mian Qaisar, 2020) has emerged, branching the research taxonomy into two classes: (1) model-based & event-driven and (2) data-driven studies. It is noticeable that event-driven based approach has a small number of studies. This approach leverages efficient online approximation of LIB event-driven modifications to improve Coulomb Counting calculations. Notably, event-driven data tracking can lead to data compression, dimensional reduction, and therefore, reducing computational complexity of models. However, as mentioned, these methods have not seen active development recently due to their limitations. Instead, data-driven methods have risen to be the primary concentration in this field recently.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Shallow learning and statistical learning methods
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, the SoH forecasting methods aim to model the capacity degradation using machine learning techniques, relying on the attributes extracted from the time-series LIB data. Significantly, analytical techniques can be employed across the entire prediction process. On one hand, this employment has proved to be effective in extracting and reproducing meaningful signals and waveforms from noisy data. Therefore, statistical attributes can be utilized for cleaning and cleansing the waveform data, which can serve as the basis for generating models. On the other hand, statistical strategies can also be applied in the later stage of SoH prediction. However, statistical methods and classical/conventional learning machines, such as Bayes Naive Classification or Particle Filter usually have common gaps including: (i) these methods generate output by employing dependencies from training and expect output in the learning period, and (ii) this learning process does not involve a great deal of hidden latent features leading to a smaller number of trainable parameters, which essentially different from deep-learning approaches. Therefore, they are commonly categorized into shallow learning group.

It is evident that the analytical approaches for this problem have been stated such as Kalman Filter (KF) (Spagnol et al., 2011), Particle Filter (PF) (Wei et al., 2018) and Single Particle Model (Pozzi et al., 2018). To accelerate the model for more accurate performance and stability, the Adaptive Hybrid model (Kim et al., 2013), and Constraint Propagation for LIB data (Locatelli et al., 2021) have been proposed. Some studies using these employment have already considered relaxation effect, such as one proposed in Pan et al. (2021).

Following the cleaning and cleansing processes, process modeling can be applied, leveraging stochastic dependencies to address the variation of data in the previous cycles such as in Si et al. (2011) and Xu et al. (2019). This approach typically involves predicting the SoH by estimating a stochastic degradation model of battery capacity (Si et al., 2011) or leveraging the Wiener Process to incorporate the relaxation effect into the model.

In recent years, several examples of shallow learning approaches have emerged. Such studies can be found in Hu et al. (2016) and Piao et al. (2016) employed Bayesian Predictive or Hidden Markov model. Alternatively, Liu et al. (2013, 2015) utilized Gaussian Process Regression and Relevance-Vector Machine as similar techniques. Notably, the paper (Zhang et al., 2022) leverages Incremental Capacity Analysis (ICA) and improved Broad Learning System (BLS) network-based SoH estimation technology to develop reliable and high-accuracy output. Despite a long list of notable achievements, shallow learning approaches generally encounter challenges dealing with huge volume datasets or high-dimensional data. It is evident that these methods in the list of shallow employment reach saturation after processing a limited training data volume. Furthermore, recent surveys across different domains suggest that shallow learning models have limited scalability because of over-fitting (Gasparetto et al., 2022; Liu and Lang, 2019). In contrast, deep learning approaches have the potential to achieve far better performance (Thompson et al., 2020). As a result, there has been a surge of recent work in this direction discussed subsequently.

In addition, DL represents another type of data-driven approach that has achieved remarkable success in variant fields including time-series forecasting. Recently, a large number of DL methods have been utilized to address the LIB SoH forecasting problem efficiently in leveraging large datasets. Chun et al. (2019) utilized CNN to approximate the parameters in an electrochemical model. In Khalid et al. (2019), a Multi-Layer Perception in a combination with an enhanced PF technique is used. In Xia and Abu Qahouq (2019), the authors proposed a FNN with online complex impedance. In Zhang et al. (2023), a model based on gradual decreasing current, double correlation analysis, and Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) has been used as a typical deep learning sequence-based model. A more advanced deep learning model called LSTM, known for sequence data, has been used in Zhang et al. (2017) for capacity prediction. In another advanced approach, Wang et al. (2023a) leveraged an adaptive LSTM Neural Network to present a robust forecasting model for the RUL of LIBs. Similarly, Wang et al. (2023b) applied stochastic Gaussian process regression using an LSTM-based approach for capacity estimation. These methods can be considered typical DL data-driven yet sequence-based forecasting techniques in the literature. Especially, Park et al. (2020) enhanced their LSTM model by incorporating multi-channel features, which demonstrated effectiveness in LIB capacity prediction.

In 2017, significant contributions to the NLP and subsequently impacted the other fields as well have been proposed in Vaswani et al. (2017). In the context of LIB capacity prediction, Transformer has also been employed to improve estimation performance. Hu and Zhao (2022) proposed a method that utilizes a technique of wavelet threshold (Luo and Zhang, 2012) to filter the data and employ the Transformer architecture for LIB capacity prediction. Chen et al. (2022) adapted the Transformer for LIB capacity prediction and employed AutoEncoder for noise filtering instead of traditional filters like Kalman Filter or Particle Filter. Their architecture, introduced as Convolutional Transformer (ConvTrans), achieved remarkable results in less training time and robustness. Another similar study that leveraged Bayesian Transformer (Li et al., 2024) also provided a recent impressive estimation using Incremental Energy Analysis. These methods have encouraged multiple LIB SoH forecasting studies using Transformer architecture recently, as subsequently discussed.

The Multi-channel LSTM (MC-LSTM) (Park et al., 2020) is possibly one of the most inspiring deep-learning studies. It employed an important sampling technique consisting of eleven (11) data points in the charging phase (Wu et al., 2016). Additionally, channel-wise attributes have been employed to generate a multivariate time series engagement, which can be efficiently addressed by an LSTM structure. Fig. 14(a) displays the input features leveraged to feed into the model. However, the MC-LSTM shows the gap in accuracy in SoH forecasting and provides room for us to improve.

ce:para.ce:bold: Deep Learning Methods — ARNS and CTARNS

ce:section.ce:para: The recent study presented in Pham et al. (2022) that adapts LSTM in their ultimate architecture but with an upgraded version, BiLSTM is one of the state-of-the-art approaches regarding precise results. On one hand, the authors leverage the stacked Sequence-to-sequence (seq2seq) architecture (Tian et al., 2021) to carefully extract the information in each cycle which is an inspiring mechanism applied for time-series data. On the other hand, they analyze the possible features of a cycle, one of which is the relaxation effect, to improve performance. The overall architecture of ARNS is demonstrated in Fig. 14(b).
ARNS presents two branches, Linear and Non-linear branches described below.

ce:list-item.ce:para:
Linear branch uses a basic autoregression model to retrieve the dependencies of LIB aging attributes, which means that its capacity increases or decreases in the near future with an appropriate gradient. The output of this branch, which is

Non-linear branch is utilized to deal with spikes or peaks, which are hard to predict if using linear methods. The strong point of this model is the employment of the relaxation factor, represented by the feature
T

Although the results obtained with ARNS are indeed accurate, the architecture faces the challenge of extended training times, a drawback often linked to LSTM. To address this limitation, a potential enhancement involves substituting LSTM with a module designed for quicker training. The Transformer architecture emerges as a replacement. In a study by the authors of Kim and Kim (2022), an advanced update of Transformer adjustment is proposed. The output obtained in their research is astonishing, as this variant outperforms well-known time series variants of Transformer such as Informer (Zhou et al., 2020) and LogSparse (Li et al., 2019) in bench-marking time-series datasets. Inspired by these results, CTARNS has been developed, which will be discussed below.

Obviously, the method based on BiLSTM in Pham et al. (2022) causes the module to consume long training time. Leveraging the Convolutional Transformer Decoder (CT-De) module suggested in 2.5, the CTARNS (Bui et al., 2023) decreases the processing time dramatically. In their Decoder, TConv is appropriate to be applied for time-series problems due to its causality and dilation due to their efficiency. Moreover, the Self-Attention is more powerful based on its parallelism. The improved architecture is demonstrated in Fig. 14(c). Apart from CT-De, other techniques such as SeqInSeq, Linear and Non-linear branches, and relaxation effect utilization are applied similar to those of ARNS (Pham et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Taxonomy of the LIB SoH forecasting problems
ce:section.ce:para: The taxonomy of related works is illustrated in Fig. 15. In our approach, we leverage the concept of channel-wise and cycle-wise input from papers (Wu et al., 2016) and Hu et al. (2016). Additionally, we incorporate the method of encoding the relaxation effect introduced in Pan et al. (2021). Our model is developed following the branch led by Multi-channel approaches described in Park et al. (2020) and Pham et al. (2022). Notably, ARNS (Pham et al., 2022) and CTARNS (Bui et al., 2023) are perhaps the state-of-the-art studies, that serve as the benchmarks to evaluate our proposed hypothesis, referred to as MvNGCN. Furthermore, we draw inspiration from the techniques employed in the field of multi-variate time-series studies, specifically from Kim and Kim (2022) and Cao et al. (2021), which are highlighted in yellow boxes in the taxonomy tree.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Multiple-View Nested Graph Convolutional Network (MvNGCN)
ce:section.ce:para: Recently, GNNs have been utilized for various time-series forecasting problems. However, previous methods often required predefined graph structures, posing challenges for practical data that lacks explicit spatial relationships. Existing approaches in the literature have proposed modeling graph structures to capture inter-series relationships explicitly. With the goal to improve the performance of the aforementioned model, we propose MvNGCN, which combines two graphs for different purposes. The first graph encodes correlations using Self-Attention from exploratory variables, while the second graph leverages correlations through another Self-Attention branch over the time steps of the LIB features. By combining the output from both graphs, the model can take advantage of the strengths of each graph, leading to improved robustness, accuracy, and reliability.

The final architecture of MvNGCN is built upon previous state-of-the-art studies and enhanced by incorporating both Local CT-De and Global CT-De modules. However, in the final version, the Global CT-De module will be substituted with the ultimate 2-view nested GCN, as illustrated in Fig. 16.

Similar to previous related methods, the overall architecture of our approach consists of linear and non-linear branches. The linear branch utilizes an Autoregression model with historical capacities serving as variables. On the other hand, the non-linear branch involves extracting Charge Profile features from each cycle and concatenating them with the corresponding cycle-wise features. This process results in a feature matrix

One of which is the Local CT-DE and the other is the 2-view GCN module. The first one involves the combination of feature matrix serves as the input for MvNGCN, which will be discussed subsequently.
In the ultimate figure, the internal structure of the Local CT-De inherits the proposal from Bui et al. (2023). Fig. 17 depicts the organization of this component.

In this Section, we will provide experimental data to demonstrate our hypothesis. To prove the capability of GCN compared to sequence-based approaches, we conducted experiments using statistical and shallow learning methods such as GF, SVR, and sequence-based methods like ARNS, CTARNS, MC-LSTM. We then compared these results with the experimental outcomes of MvNGCN. The tables below present comprehensive scenarios for these comparisons across different datasets. Firstly, we introduce the data preprocessing.

Basically, data cleaning is conducted following the similar procedure outlined in Bui et al. (2023). To obtain the capacity data for every charging phase, the datasets are arranged in chronological order. Missing attributes are filled based on the mean of the preceding n data points. The constant n ( = 5 in our case) is selected in the charging and discharging processes. Later on, standard normalization is applied to reduce the imbalance in different features. In more detail, min–max normalization technique is used, given in Eq. (33). This normalization process was executed prior to data separation into different train/validation/and test segments.

To improve data stability, the Z-score (Aggarwal et al., 2019) abnormality filtering technique is applied. Firstly, we calculate Z-score using Eq. (34).

) separately. Statistically, nearly 5% of the existing data are eliminated in this process. By experimental investigation, this portion and aforementioned configuration contribute stably to the output of our models.

To properly evaluate our model, we employed a cross-validation separation over the datasets. We divide the training and validation segments according to a 90:10 ratio. In more details, in the NASA dataset, a testing portion for battery #5 is built based on #6, #7, and #18 as the training and validation sets, maintaining the 90:10 proportion. Similarly, for battery #6, batteries #5, #7, and #18 were used as the training and validation sets. Fig. 19 provides an illustration of this process for batteries #5 and #6 in the NASA dataset. The validation process for the NASA and CALCE datasets is summarized in Table 4.

To prove a fair performance comparison and stability analysis among the models, we employ the cross-validation technique introduced in Section 5.3, repeated n times with n different seeds (
n = 10
). These parameters were proposed originally in MC-LSTM (Park et al., 2020), in DeTransformer (Kim and Kim, 2022), and CTARNS (Bui et al., 2023). Then, we experimentally compared various tuning scenarios to find the optimal set of parameters. We used grid-search as it is relatively effective considering the different parameter settings in the experiments. Additionally, regarding graph parameters in MvNGCN, we set the hidden dimension as
d = 32
for the GCN layer and
d = 16
for the fully connected layer. These root parameters of GCN are collected in Table 5.

The quantitative output of the proposed model against existing methods will be presented subsequently. We measure the output of existing methods such as statistical or shallow learning (Gaussian fitting — GF (Liu et al., 2013), Support Vector Regression — SVR (Yang et al., 2018)) or data-driven deep learning studies (MLP (Khalid et al., 2019), LSTM (Zhang et al., 2017), MC-LSTM (Park et al., 2020), ARNS (Pham et al., 2022), CTARNS (Bui et al., 2023), DeTransformer (Kim and Kim, 2022)) for comparison purposes. The evaluation is performed on both the NASA and CALCE datasets. The query and key matrices are adjusted using the formula
Q =
W
Q
X
, where
W
Q
represents a squared matrix. The baseline models chosen for comparison are ARNS and CTARNS. The overall outputs for the NASA and CALCE datasets, averaged across the datasets and encompassing both peak and non-peak testing segments, are presented in Tables 6 and 7, respectively.

The results show that on the NASA dataset, which is most challenging due to high spikes in capacity degradation, MvNGCN outperformed all previous models. Specifically, MvNGCN overall performed approximately 7.91% RMSE and 16.78% MAPE better than the baseline, CTARNS. Moreover, MvNGCN created an enhancement of 5.43% RMSE and 12.84% MAPE on the peak test set and 13.93% RMSE and 20.38% MAPE on the non-peak test set. This proves that combining the views of modeling time step relationships and variable interactions explicitly helps enhance performance.

On the CALCE dataset, MvNGCN exhibits stability and robustness in 4 out of 6 statistical metrics. The remaining 2 measurements show outputs that are very close to the best results obtained. Specifically, MvNGCN achieves an overall improvement of 2.92% and 5.09% on overall, 11.60% improvement in Peak MAPE, a 12.87% improvement in Non-peak RMSE, and a 14.83% improvement in Non-peak MAPE, when compared to CTARNS.

Tables 8 and 9 present examples of output reports for individual batteries. Across these examples, our method consistently showcases greater stability and robustness when compared to existing methods, even considering the large volume of data and diversity of battery datasets involved.

We also carried out experiments to compare the accuracy of the following modules used for multivariate time series data, including STGCN (Yu et al., 2018), StemGNN (Cao et al., 2021), and three versions of Multiview GCN (Channel-view only, Cycle-view only, and MvNGCN version). The experiments are conducted by replacing the MvNGCN in the architecture with the other aforementioned models in turn. We perform each module evaluation on the NASA dataset. The outputs are presented in Table 10. This table again proves the robustness of the GCN module on the large dataset, compared to STGCN and StemGNN.

LIBs are widely used across various domains, particularly in 5G, Edge Computing, and Mobility applications. Due to their capacity degradation over time, accurate tracking of battery health is in high demand. Recent advancements in sequence-based deep learning models have facilitated high-precision forecasting LIB SoH. However, as mentioned in previous Sections, the recent sequence-based SoH forecasting methods have limitations in accuracy and stability. In this paper, by harnessing the data-driven GCN approach within LIBs data, we propose a sophisticated graph-based network model that delivers highly accurate and reliable predictions for LIB SoH. Through comprehensive evaluations on large datasets, our method consistently exceeds state-of-the-art results in various settings. Notably, the main contributions can be listed as follows.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Robustness: Due to the inherent nature of battery data being SeqInSeq and bearing a strong resemblance to the neighbor mechanism of nodes in a Graph, we designed nested GCNs with a specific topology to fully leverage the message passing between nodes in both a channel-wise and cycle-wise manner. This approach helps us achieve robustness in the output, consistently obtaining very low loss and high accuracy levels.

Reliability: Notably, in a GCN, the weight distribution initialization during training produces a message propagation to aggregate node information from neighboring nodes. In contrast to the sequence-based mechanisms of existing methods, our Graph model ensures a small distribution variance during weight passing. This reduces reliance on random weight initialization during training compared with the existing methods in the literature. Consequently, our model consistently delivers stable results across multiple runs (with different seeds) in experiments, ensuring its reliability.

ce:section.ce:para: In addition to the main contributions mentioned above, we incorporate powerful techniques for LIB SoH forecasting problems, including channel-wise and cycle-wise inputs, SeqInSeq engagement, peak and non-peak separation and encoding, relaxation effect, Self-Attention, and TConv, into our end-to-end prediction model. All these elements combine to create a robust end-to-end SoH forecasting approach. Compared to the baseline (Bui et al., 2023) on the NASA dataset, our method achieves a remarkable reduction of 7.91% in RMSE and 16.78% in MAPE. Specifically, MvNGCN cuts down RMSE by nearly 5.43% and 13.93%, along with a decrease of 12.84% and 20.38% in MAPE, for the separated peak and non-peak test sets. On the CALCE dataset, our method exhibits a similar trend with a decrease of 2.92% in RMSE and 5.09% in MAPE overall, and reductions of 2.32% in RMSE and 11.60% in MAPE as well as 12.87% in RMSE and 14.83% in MAPE for the peak and non-peak test sets. Overall, our method consistently outperforms various similar settings and models, making our output highly reliable in specific applications.

Notably, our method is commonly associated with DL approaches, which often exhibit higher memory consumption and longer training times. Additionally, it is widely recognized that DL models tend to underperform machine learning methods when the dataset size is small, which is considered a general drawback of DL approaches. However, DL methods have shown robustness and effectiveness when applied to large-scale datasets encompassing various domains and situations (Schulz et al., 2020). In our case, similar to the situation reported in the literature (Section 3.3.3), we are using the NASA and CALCE datasets, which have demonstrated that their data volumes are well-suited for deep learning approaches, rendering them suitable and efficient for our experiments.

It is also important to note that the problem addressed in this paper does not involve battery packs (i.e., forecasting for multiple battery cells). To extend this research to account for such dependencies, models that capture the inter-correlation between multiple battery cells will be required. We have included this as part of our future work. Additionally, other potential future approaches can also be anticipated. For example, expanding our evaluation to include randomized datasets would produce a beneficial understanding of our model generalizability. Furthermore, the approach can be focused on fine-tuning and improvement of the data-driven topology in the ultimate model to better align with the natural LIB data dependencies.
